# **Architectural Synthesis and Implementation Blueprint: Integrating OpenAlgo, VectorBT, and Sector Rotation via Dhan V2 APIs in a Hybrid Stack Ecosystem**

The contemporary landscape of algorithmic trading infrastructure demands an uncompromising synthesis of high-frequency data ingestion, instantaneous order execution, and sophisticated mathematical backtesting. The central objective of this exhaustive technical analysis is to architect a foundational blueprint and a comprehensive deployment prompt for "Project-R," a web application inherently structured upon a MERN stack (MongoDB, Express.js, React, Node.js).1 The architectural mandate requires the seamless integration of three distinct, highly specialized Python-based quantitative frameworks—specifically, the OpenAlgo execution engine 2, the VectorBT backtesting repository 3, and Sector Rotation Map logic relying on Relative Rotation Graphs (RRG).4 These sophisticated computational engines must interface seamlessly with the high-performance data streams and execution endpoints provided by the Dhan V2 API.6

Constructing this infrastructure requires traversing the inherent friction between Node.js, an asynchronous, event-driven JavaScript runtime tailored for scalable I/O operations, and Python, a synchronous environment that overwhelmingly dominates the quantitative finance and machine learning ecosystems.8 This document provides a meticulous deconstruction of the required microservices architecture, mathematical modeling techniques, API routing protocols, and frontend visualization strategies necessary to construct a latency-optimized, broker-agnostic algorithmic trading dashboard.

## **1\. The Hybrid Architectural Paradigm: Bridging Node.js and Python**

The fundamental engineering challenge of Project-R lies in the technological divergence between the existing application stack and the targeted quantitative repositories. Node.js natively excels at handling thousands of concurrent connections, rendering it the ideal candidate for managing WebSocket connections, routing API gateway requests, and serving the React frontend.8 However, Node.js is fundamentally unsuited for the intense, synchronous CPU loads demanded by vectorized matrix computations over large datasets, such as those performed by VectorBT across five years of historical minute-by-minute trading data.8 Conversely, while Python provides unrivaled libraries such as Pandas, NumPy, and Numba for mathematical processing, deploying it directly as a primary web server to handle simultaneous live data streaming can introduce concurrency bottlenecks due to the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL).8

To resolve this dichotomy, the system must deploy a hybrid microservices architecture that strategically delegates responsibilities based on runtime strengths. This design isolates the heavy quantitative computation and broker connectivity into a dedicated Python microservice while maintaining the Node.js and Express server as the primary orchestration layer and client-facing API gateway.11

| Architectural Component | Technology Stack | Designated System Functionality |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Frontend Interface** | React, Next.js, Tailwind CSS, TypeScript | User authentication UI, strategy visualization, interactive RRG Cartesian plotting, and responsive state management.1 |
| **API Gateway & Orchestrator** | Node.js, Express.js | JWT validation, client request routing, rate-limiting, WebSocket distribution to the client browser, and MongoDB orchestration.1 |
| **Persistent Storage Layer** | MongoDB, Mongoose ORM | Storing encrypted user profiles, persistent backtest metadata, saved strategy configurations, and historical execution logs. |
| **Quantitative Microservice** | Python 3.11+, FastAPI / Flask | OpenAlgo broker routing execution, VectorBT vectorized computations, and mathematical normalization for Sector Rotation Maps.2 |
| **Inter-Process Communication** | ZeroMQ (ZMQ), Redis | Ultra-low latency communication bridging Node.js and Python, background task queuing for prolonged backtests, and WebSocket payload normalization.2 |

The communication pipeline within this hybrid topology requires rigorous structuring. When a user interacts with the React frontend to initiate a backtest, the request is routed through the Express.js gateway. The Express server validates the user's JSON Web Token (JWT), queries MongoDB for the user's specific Dhan API credentials, and constructs a sanitized payload.1 This payload cannot be passed to the Python microservice via standard synchronous HTTP REST calls if the backtest is anticipated to take more than a few seconds, as this would block the Node.js event loop or trigger gateway timeouts. Instead, the architecture utilizes a message broker—either Redis or ZeroMQ—to place the backtest request into an asynchronous task queue.2

The Python quantitative service, operating independently, dequeues the task, utilizes the OpenAlgo framework to authenticate with the Dhan API, fetches the requisite historical data, and executes the VectorBT calculations.2 Upon completion, the Python service publishes the results—comprising a JSON summary and a generated QuantStats HTML tearsheet—back to the message broker. The Node.js server consumes this result and pushes it to the waiting React client via a continuous Socket.io connection, establishing a non-blocking, highly responsive user experience.2

## **2\. DhanHQ V2 API Mechanics and High-Frequency Data Ingestion**

The lifeblood of any quantitative framework is the accuracy, depth, and resolution of its historical and live market data. Project-R relies exclusively on the DhanHQ Ver 2.0 API, a state-of-the-art REST-like platform that facilitates both live execution and profound historical data retrieval.6 The structural integration of Dhan's data endpoints requires meticulous payload formatting and an understanding of the chronological constraints of market data architectures.

### **2.1 Historical Data Retrieval Constraints and Parameterization**

The foundation of the VectorBT backtesting engine and the Relative Rotation Graphs requires exhaustive historical OHLCV (Open, High, Low, Close, Volume) data. The Dhan V2 API caters to this necessity through distinct routing endpoints optimized for different temporal resolutions. For daily macroeconomic analysis—such as calculating long-term sector momentum over a 252-day trading year—the system queries the /charts/historical endpoint.6 For micro-structural algorithmic strategies like intraday supertrends, the system queries the /charts/intraday endpoint, which provides granular candlestick data at 1, 5, 15, 25, and 60-minute intervals.6

With the release of Dhan API Version 2.2, the data ingestion capabilities have been massively expanded. The platform now permits the retrieval of up to five years of minute-wise intraday historical data for all NSE, BSE, and MCX instruments.10 Furthermore, the API now provides Open Interest (OI) data for futures and options instruments, a critical metric for algorithmic models that measure market liquidity and derivative positioning.10

To interact with these endpoints, the Python microservice must construct a strictly typed POST payload containing the following deterministic parameters:

| API Payload Parameter | Required Data Type | Architectural Implementation Description |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| securityId | String | The unique numerical identifier for the instrument, meticulously mapped and cross-referenced from Dhan's downloadable api-scrip-master.csv file.15 |
| exchangeSegment | Enum | Defines the market category, requiring strict adherence to Dhan's enumeration standards (e.g., NSE\_EQ for equities, NSE\_FNO for derivatives).6 |
| instrument | Enum | The classification of the financial asset (e.g., EQUITY, FUTIDX, OPTSTK) allowing the backend to route requests to the correct data silos.6 |
| fromDate / toDate | String | The chronological boundary of the backtest or analysis, formatted precisely to standard date strings.6 |
| oi | Boolean | An optional flag introduced in Version 2.2 that instructs the Dhan servers to append Open Interest values to the returned derivative candlesticks.6 |

### **2.2 Data Transformation and Vectorized Formatting**

The raw response returned by the Dhan V2 API presents a significant data engineering hurdle. The data is delivered as a structured JSON object containing parallel arrays of integers and floats, wherein the timestamps are provided as standard Unix epoch integers.16 Directly feeding this raw JSON into the VectorBT engine is computationally prohibitive and structurally invalid.

Upon receipt of the API payload, the Python microservice must immediately execute a transformation pipeline. The parallel arrays must be zipped and ingested into a Pandas DataFrame.17 Subsequently, the epoch timestamps must be passed through a vectorized chronological converter to establish a standard Pandas DatetimeIndex localized to the Asia/Kolkata timezone.17 This specific localization ensures that intraday algorithms utilizing precise temporal windows (e.g., executing only between 09:30 AM and 03:00 PM) operate synchronously with actual Indian stock market hours.3 Only after this rigorous cleaning and indexing process is the DataFrame deemed ready for consumption by the downstream mathematical engines.

## **3\. The OpenAlgo Abstraction Layer: Neutralizing Vendor Lock-In**

While integrating directly with the Dhan API is technically feasible, hardcoding direct broker dependencies into Project-R introduces severe architectural fragility. Broker APIs frequently deprecate legacy endpoints, alter required payload structures, or introduce entirely new authentication flows, such as Dhan's transition to supporting static IP bindings.18 Maintaining continuous parity with these proprietary changes absorbs vast amounts of development capital. To neutralize this vendor lock-in, Project-R incorporates the OpenAlgo framework as an abstraction bridge.2

OpenAlgo is a self-hosted algorithmic trading platform built on a Python Flask backend that acts as a universal translator for over 30 Indian brokers.2 By embedding the OpenAlgo core within the Python microservice, Project-R interacts exclusively with OpenAlgo's unified REST API layer (/api/v1/), standardizing complex order management across 30+ endpoints for placing, modifying, and canceling orders.2

### **3.1 ZeroMQ Messaging and Real-Time WebSocket Streaming**

The latency introduced by standard HTTP polling for live market updates is unacceptable in quantitative systems. OpenAlgo circumvents this by implementing a unified WebSocket proxy server, operating on port 8765, which utilizes the ZeroMQ (ZMQ) protocol.2 ZeroMQ is an ultra-high-performance asynchronous messaging library designed for distributed concurrent applications. It abstracts the complexities of raw TCP sockets into sophisticated routing patterns like Publish/Subscribe (Pub/Sub).

Within Project-R, the OpenAlgo engine establishes a direct WebSocket stream with Dhan's servers to receive live tick data. OpenAlgo then normalizes this proprietary tick stream and publishes it onto the internal ZeroMQ message bus.2 The Node.js server subscribes to this local ZMQ bus, ingesting the normalized pricing data in microseconds, and immediately broadcasts it to the React frontend via Socket.IO, resulting in a perfectly synchronized live market dashboard that remains entirely decoupled from Dhan's specific WebSocket implementation.2

### **3.2 Advanced Analytics and API Analyzer Mode**

OpenAlgo provides vast auxiliary functionalities that profoundly upgrade the capabilities of Project-R. The recent Version 2.0.0.1 release introduced a comprehensive Option Chain Analytics suite.19 This allows the Python backend to request calculated Option Greeks (Implied Volatility, Delta, Theta, Vega, Gamma), access a Gamma Exposure (GEX) dashboard, compute Max Pain levels, and construct 3D Volatility Surfaces natively without requiring external quantitative libraries.19

Furthermore, OpenAlgo features a complete pre-deployment testing environment dubbed the "API Analyzer Mode".2 This sandbox provides the user with ₹1 Crore in virtual capital, operating within an isolated database environment that simulates realistic limit and stop-loss order fills, margin systems, and leverage.2 This allows users of Project-R to paper-trade their sophisticated VectorBT strategies in real-time market conditions before committing live capital to the Dhan API, drastically reducing user risk profiles.

## **4\. VectorBT: The Apex Vectorized Backtesting Engine**

The core value proposition of Project-R lies in its capacity to rigorously validate algorithmic trading hypotheses. Traditional retail backtesting libraries employ event-driven architectures that utilize standard for-loops to iterate through historical time series data day by day. While logically intuitive, this methodology scales catastrophically poorly when a user attempts to optimize a strategy across thousands of parameter permutations.13

To overcome this, the architecture incorporates the framework established in the marketcalls/vectorbt-backtesting-skills repository.3 VectorBT is engineered upon Pandas, NumPy, and the Numba Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler.13 Instead of iterating over events, VectorBT translates historical price data and trading logic into multi-dimensional NumPy arrays.21 It performs operations on the entire dataset simultaneously utilizing vectorized matrix mathematics, resulting in computation speeds that are up to 100 times faster than conventional Python backtesters.13

### **4.1 Implementation of Pre-Built Algorithmic Templates**

The integrated repository provides 12 distinct, highly evolved strategy templates spanning diverse quantitative methodologies, which must be natively accessible via the React frontend.3

1. **Trend-Following Paradigms:** The engine supports standard Exponential Moving Average (EMA) crossovers, optimized for 10-period and 20-period intersections.3 More advanced systems include the Supertrend strategy, specifically tailored for the Indian market by incorporating strict intraday session boundaries—entering trades only after 09:30 AM and enforcing a mandatory algorithmic square-off at 15:15 PM to avoid overnight gap risks.3 Additionally, the SDA2 trend system deploys Weighted Moving Averages (WMA) enveloped within Standard Deviation and Average True Range (ATR) bands to identify explosive price breakouts.3  
2. **Mean-Reversion and Rotational Models:** The engine natively supports the Relative Strength Index (RSI), utilizing standard 14-period lookbacks with 30/70 thresholds to trigger overbought and oversold mean-reversion signals.3 For portfolio-level algorithms, the Dual Momentum strategy facilitates quarterly rotation between inversely correlated Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs), seamlessly switching capital allocation between assets like NIFTYBEES and GOLDBEES based on comparative trailing momentum.3  
3. **Specialized Accumulation and Breakouts:** The Donchian Channel algorithm tracks multi-week high/low breakouts, specifically engineered with shifted data arrays to unequivocally prevent lookahead bias—a common flaw in retail algorithmic design.3 Furthermore, the RSI Accumulation model allows for slab-wise capital deployment, algorithmically buying 5%, 10%, or 20% of a portfolio allocation as the RSI indicator progressively plummets into deep oversold territory.3

### **4.2 Realistic Indian Transactional Cost Modeling**

A backtest that ignores the friction of the real market is a dangerous illusion. A strategy demonstrating a 40% annual return may result in a net loss when execution costs are factored in. The integrated framework enforces broker-neutral but structurally accurate transaction cost modeling specific to the Indian market.3 When the Python service initiates the vectorbt.Portfolio.from\_signals matrix constructor, it meticulously applies a 4-segment deduction model representing Slippage, Brokerage, Securities Transaction Tax (STT), Exchange Turnover Charges, and GST.3

| Trading Segment | Applied Algorithmic Fee Structure | Justification and Composition |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Equity Delivery** | 0.111% total impact cost | Accounts for elevated STT on overnight holdings and standard depository participant charges.3 |
| **Equity Intraday** | 0.0225% total impact cost | Lower STT footprint due to the lack of overnight delivery, but high frequency necessitates precise slippage modeling.3 |
| **Index/Stock Futures** | 0.018% total impact cost | Reflects the highly leveraged nature of futures contracts where exchange fees are minimized relative to notional value.3 |
| **Options Contracts** | 0.098% total impact cost | Applies to the premium turnover value, incorporating SEBI's revised lot sizes (e.g., Nifty adjusted to 65, BankNifty to 30\) effective December 31, 2025\.3 |

### **4.3 Robustness Validation and Output Generation**

Following the execution of the vectorized logic, the Python backend must format the immense array of data into digestible metrics for the Node.js API gateway. The system produces a professional-grade QuantStats HTML tearsheet.3 This document contains over 30 profound metrics, including monthly performance heatmaps, trailing drawdown profiles, and the Sharpe Ratio—a critical measure of risk-adjusted return where any value above 1.0 is considered decent, and above 2.0 is deemed exceptional.3

To prevent data-mining bias and curve-fitting, the Python microservice executes Walk-Forward Analysis (WFE).3 This process continuously rolls a training window and a testing window forward through the historical data. By optimizing the strategy on the in-sample training data and validating it against the unseen out-of-sample test data, the algorithm generates a Walk-Forward Efficiency score, empirically proving whether the strategy can adapt to novel market conditions.3 The Node.js backend retrieves the static HTML tearsheet path alongside a translated "Plain-Language Report" mapping total strategy returns against the NIFTY 50 benchmark alpha, and pushes this JSON directly to the React interface for seamless presentation.3

## **5\. Sector Rotation Map (RRG) Mathematical Modeling**

While VectorBT focuses on micro-structural algorithmic execution, the Sector Rotation Map provides a macroeconomic visualization of institutional capital flow. Originating from the concept of Relative Rotation Graphs (RRG), this visual tool maps the relative strength and momentum of an array of peripheral assets—typically sectoral indices—against a centralized benchmark.4 By integrating the logic from the marketcalls/sector-rotation-map repository, Project-R empowers users to visualize where the market is currently deploying capital before an algorithmic signal even triggers.5

### **5.1 Data Acquisition and Index Mapping**

The Sector Rotation Map exclusively tracks indices rather than individual equities. To facilitate this, the Python microservice must parse Dhan's API scrip master to locate the specific numerical IDs corresponding to the primary NSE indices.15 The system fetches trailing daily historical data for the absolute benchmark—the NIFTY 50 24—and cross-analyzes it against a curated list of secondary sectoral indices, which includes Nifty Bank, Nifty IT, Nifty Auto, Nifty FMCG, Nifty Pharma, Nifty Energy, and Nifty Metal.24

### **5.2 Mathematical Formulation of Cartesian Coordinates**

The RRG is rendered on a Cartesian plane consisting of four distinct quadrants: Leading (Top-Right), Weakening (Bottom-Right), Lagging (Bottom-Left), and Improving (Top-Left).26 The precise placement of a sector within this plane requires sophisticated, normalized mathematical transformations processed via Pandas DataFrames.4

**Step 1: The Price Relative Calculation** The foundational bedrock of the algorithm is the raw price relative, calculated by dividing the closing price of the target sectoral index by the closing price of the NIFTY 50 benchmark.22 This strips away the absolute direction of the overall market, isolating pure relative outperformance.

![][image1]  
**Step 2: JdK RS-Ratio (The X-Axis)** The JdK RS-Ratio dictates the horizontal placement on the graph and represents the primary trend of relative performance.4 Mathematically, it is a normalized, smoothed moving average of the Price Relative calculated over a specific lookback window (typically 12 to 14 weeks). When the RS-Ratio traverses above the 100-line, it explicitly mathematically confirms that the sector has entered a macro relative uptrend against the broader market.22

**Step 3: JdK RS-Momentum (The Y-Axis)** Because the RS-Ratio relies on moving averages, it inherently suffers from chronological lag.22 To counteract this, the JdK RS-Momentum calculates the rate of change of the RS-Ratio, acting as an early-warning leading indicator for the Y-Axis.4 This is mathematically derived using rolling logarithmic returns, assessing the momentum variance between a long-period and a short-period lookback.5

![][image2]  
Where ![][image3] represents the rolling return of the RS-Ratio over a longer window (e.g., 12 weeks), and ![][image4] represents the rolling return over a shorter interval (e.g., 1 week).5

**Step 4: Z-Score Standardization** The raw outputs of the RS-Ratio and RS-Momentum fluctuate across vastly different numerical scales depending on the inherent volatility of the underlying sector. To plot these disparate metrics cohesively onto a single, centralized graph where the nexus is perfectly aligned at the coordinates (100, 100), the data arrays must undergo rigorous Z-Score Standardization.5

![][image5]  
Where ![][image6] is the rolling mean and ![][image7] is the rolling standard deviation. The finalized, normalized coordinate is then derived by applying a unified scaling factor:

![][image8]

### **5.3 Frontend Visualization and Plotly.js Integration**

Once the Python microservice completes the vectorized computation of the normalized arrays, it returns a dense JSON payload to the Node.js Express server.26 This payload contains the trailing historical coordinates (typically the last 5 to 10 weeks) for every requested sector. The React frontend consumes this data using the plotly.js-react charting library.26 The frontend iterates through the data arrays, plotting the historical trajectory—known as the "tail"—of each sector.26 This visual tail demonstrates the clockwise rotational trajectory of capital as it migrates from the 'Improving' quadrant, peaks in the 'Leading' quadrant, deteriorates into 'Weakening', bottoms out in 'Lagging', and eventually curves back upward, establishing an intuitive, instantly recognizable macroeconomic map.22

## **6\. The Deep Prompt Architecture for AI Code Generation**

The orchestration of a MERN stack heavily integrated with specialized Python quantitative logic is an extraordinarily complex software engineering task. Modern development pipelines leverage Large Language Model (LLM) coding agents (such as Cursor, GitHub Copilot, or Claude) to accelerate boilerplate generation and algorithmic transcription.2 To ensure the AI agent adheres strictly to the hybrid topology, mathematical constraints, and explicit API parameters defined in this document, a highly structured, rigid "Deep Prompt" must be provided.

The following text block represents the literal, meticulously engineered instructions that must be injected into the AI coding agent to generate the foundational source code for Project-R.

---

---

**Role Definition:** You are an elite Full-Stack Quantitative Systems Architect. Your expertise lies in distributed microservices combining a MERN stack (MongoDB, Express, React, Node.js) with Python-based high-frequency financial infrastructure (FastAPI, Pandas, NumPy, VectorBT, ZeroMQ).

**Project Objective:** You are tasked with writing the comprehensive codebase for "Project-R," a self-hosted algorithmic trading, backtesting, and macroeconomic visualization dashboard. The platform incorporates logic synthesized from three specific GitHub frameworks: marketcalls/openalgo, marketcalls/vectorbt-backtesting-skills, and marketcalls/sector-rotation-map.

**Mandatory Architectural Constraints:**

1. **Frontend:** Develop using React (Next.js framework), TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, ShadcnUI for modular components, plotly.js-react for Cartesian charting, and xyflow/React Flow for visual strategy building.  
2. **API Gateway / Primary Backend:** Develop using Node.js and Express.js. Use Mongoose ORM for MongoDB schema management. This layer strictly handles JWT-based user authentication, session state, REST API routing, and WebSocket broadcasting via Socket.io.  
3. **Quantitative Microservice:** Develop using Python 3.11+ and FastAPI. This isolated service executes all heavy mathematical computations, handles Pandas DataFrame manipulations, and manages raw broker connections.  
4. **Broker Connectivity:** The platform exclusively uses the DhanHQ V2 APIs via the embedded OpenAlgo unified abstraction layer.  
5. **Inter-Process Communication (IPC):** Use ZeroMQ (ZMQ) and Redis to facilitate ultra-low latency, non-blocking asynchronous communication between the Node.js gateway and the Python quantitative engine.

**Phase 1 Execution: Python Quantitative Microservice (FastAPI \+ OpenAlgo \+ VectorBT \+ RRG)**

Write the complete Python FastAPI application structure, including main.py and modular router files, satisfying the following exact specifications:

* **OpenAlgo Broker Initialization:** Instantiate the OpenAlgo core engine. Implement a secure service to ingest encrypted Dhan API credentials (ClientID, Access Token) passed from the Node.js server. Expose endpoints that utilize OpenAlgo's unified /api/v1/ routes to fetch live account margins, Option Greeks, and Gamma Exposure (GEX) data. Initialize the ZeroMQ unified WebSocket proxy on port 8765 to normalize Dhan's live tick data into a standard Pub/Sub stream.  
* **Dhan V2 Data Ingestion Pipeline:** Write a highly optimized data service that directly interfaces with Dhan's /charts/historical and /charts/intraday endpoints. The function must accept securityId, exchangeSegment (e.g., NSE\_EQ), instrument, fromDate, toDate, and the oi (Open Interest) boolean flag. Crucially, write the transformation logic to convert the returned JSON arrays of integer epoch timestamps into a Pandas DataFrame utilizing a properly localized Asia/Kolkata DatetimeIndex.  
* **VectorBT Engine API (/api/backtest):** Write a POST route that accepts JSON parameters for a backtest request. Implement the vectorized logic for three specific templates utilizing NumPy/Numba acceleration:  
  1. *Supertrend Intraday:* Implement logic restricting entries to a 09:30 to 15:00 window, enforcing a mandatory algorithmic square-off at 15:15.  
  2. *RSI Mean Reversion:* Utilize a standard 14-period lookback with mathematically strict 30/70 thresholds for accumulation and distribution.  
  3. *Dual Momentum ETF Rotation:* Implement rotational logic scoring relative momentum between NIFTYBEES and GOLDBEES.  
     *Crucial Math Constraint:* You MUST instantiate the vectorbt.Portfolio.from\_signals constructor utilizing the precise Indian 4-segment transaction cost model: Equity Delivery (0.111%), Equity Intraday (0.0225%), Futures (0.018%), Options (0.098%). Implement logic to apply SEBI's revised December 2025 lot sizes (e.g., Nifty=65).  
     *Output Generation:* The endpoint must generate a comprehensive QuantStats HTML tearsheet saved to a static volume, and return a JSON payload containing exactly: Total Return, Sharpe Ratio, Max Drawdown, CAGR, and Benchmark Alpha relative to the NIFTY 50\.  
* **Sector Rotation Map API (/api/rrg):** Write a GET route calculating macroeconomic sector rotation based on Relative Rotation Graphs.  
  *Input Data:* Fetch the trailing 252 days of daily closing prices for the NIFTY 50 benchmark and ten primary Nifty sectoral indices (e.g., Nifty Bank, Nifty IT).  
  *Mathematical Implementation:* Calculate the absolute Price Relative. Calculate the JdK RS-Ratio as the normalized moving average of the Price Relative. Calculate the JdK RS-Momentum using rolling logarithmic returns between a parameterized long-period and short-period. Finally, apply a rigorous Z-Score Standardization algorithm to ensure the coordinate nexus centers perfectly at (100, 100).  
  *Output:* Return a nested JSON array mapping each sector to its trailing 10-week historical (X, Y) coordinate points to allow the frontend to render the visual "tails".

**Phase 2 Execution: Node.js / Express API Gateway and Orchestration**

Write the complete Express server application (server.js, controllers, middleware, and Mongoose schemas):

* Implement JWT validation middleware securing all quantitative routes.  
* Define the Mongoose schema BacktestLog. The schema must definitively record: userId, timestamp, strategyName, targetAsset, appliedCapital, cagr, maxDrawdown, sharpeRatio, and the relative path to the generated QuantStats static HTML file.  
* Implement asynchronous request handling. When the React frontend requests a heavy VectorBT backtest, the Express route must push the job payload into a Redis task queue, return a jobId immediately to the client to prevent HTTP timeouts, and utilize Socket.IO to emit an asynchronous job\_complete event containing the JSON results once the Python worker finishes the task. Subscribe the Node.js application to the Python ZeroMQ Publisher socket to securely ingest real-time tick data and proxy it to the frontend via WebSockets.

**Phase 3 Execution: React / Next.js Frontend Visualization**

Write the highly responsive React components to consume the API Gateway:

* **Component 1: Algorithmic Configuration Form.** Construct a ShadcnUI form allowing the user to select predefined tickers, a VectorBT strategy template, chronological date boundaries, and initial virtual capital. Implement asynchronous UI state management showing a loading skeleton while polling for the backend jobId completion status.  
* **Component 2: QuantStats Dashboard.** Render the summarized JSON financial metrics in dynamic KPI cards using Tailwind CSS. Directly below, embed the generated Python QuantStats HTML tearsheet securely within an \<iframe\> to prevent global CSS collisions.  
* **Component 3: RRG Cartesian Plotting.** Instantiate a plotly.js-react scatter plot divided distinctly into four uniquely colored quadrants: Improving (Top-Left), Leading (Top-Right), Weakening (Bottom-Right), and Lagging (Bottom-Left). Consume the JSON array from the /api/rrg route. Iterate iteratively over the historical coordinate arrays to draw smooth, interconnected SVG lines representing the trailing historical "tails" of each sector, terminating with a bold, distinct graphical marker indicating the most recent date's coordinate.

**Code Quality and Defensive Programming Mandates:**

* Implement ubiquitous error handling: utilize strict try/except blocks in Python and try/catch wrappers in Node.js.  
* Enforce rigorous payload validation typing: utilize Pydantic models in the FastAPI layer and Zod schemas in the Express layer.  
* Strictly decouple algorithmic and mathematical logic from the HTTP route handlers. Ensure functions are pure and highly testable.

## ---

---

**7\. Strategic Implications and Advanced Ripple Effects**

The meticulous execution of this hybrid quantitative architecture yields profound second-order ripple effects that elevate Project-R far beyond standard retail trading platforms. The integration of these specific libraries creates an ecosystem where the whole drastically outperforms the sum of its independent parts.

The most significant strategic implication is the complete neutralization of architectural obsolescence. By refusing to wire the React/Node.js frontend directly to the Dhan APIs, and instead utilizing the Python-based OpenAlgo abstraction layer, the platform achieves complete broker neutrality.2 If, in the future, the Dhan API undergoes a catastrophic deprecation of its Version 2 endpoints, or if a user wishes to migrate their capital entirely to a competing brokerage such as Zerodha or Fyers, the core mathematical logic within Project-R remains flawlessly untouched.2 The OpenAlgo layer simply requires a reconfiguration of the authentication handshake, allowing decades of proprietary algorithmic development and machine learning models to persist without requiring massive code refactoring.

Furthermore, the implementation of Relative Rotation Graphs alongside VectorBT enables a compound synergy previously reserved for institutional hedge funds. Standard retail algorithms are inherently myopic, focusing on the isolated micro-structure of a single asset's price chart. By incorporating the Sector Rotation Map, users are granted macroeconomic vision.4 They can visually identify the precise moment institutional liquidity begins flowing out of an overbought sector (evidenced by the RS-Momentum vector plunging into the 'Weakening' quadrant) and flooding into an undervalued sector traversing the 'Improving' quadrant.22 The user can then immediately deploy the VectorBT Dual Momentum ETF Rotation template to algorithmically execute trades capitalizing on this newly identified macroeconomic capital migration.3 This seamless transition from high-level visual thesis generation into immediate, vectorized, latency-optimized market execution represents the pinnacle of modern hybrid quantitative software engineering.

9. New to web dev – do people mix Node.js with Python (e.g. for AI stuff)? \- Reddit, accessed on March 8, 2026, [https://www.reddit.com/r/node/comments/1o0hdw6/new\_to\_web\_dev\_do\_people\_mix\_nodejs\_with\_python/](https://www.reddit.com/r/node/comments/1o0hdw6/new_to_web_dev_do_people_mix_nodejs_with_python/)
